article_title: SENOMAC's Mature Survival Data at ASCO 2026 Confirms Many Breast Cancer Patients Can Safely Skip Axillary Dissection
summary: Substantively accurate clinical report; every headline/lead statistic verified verbatim against fetchable allowlisted snapshots. One body-level cite (mastectomy/T3 broadened populations) points to the wrong CancerNetwork article — the fact is true and verifiable in the other cited sources. Filed as a correction.

findings.message: Body cite for the 'broadened populations (mastectomy, larger T3 tumors)' claim points to the wrong CancerNetwork article.
findings.details: In 'Why It Matters', the claim that the findings broaden the evidence base to include mastectomy recipients and larger T3 tumors is attributed to CancerNetwork's 'senomac-reveals-omitting-alnd-yields-non-inferior-survival-in-breast-cancer' (source-1). That snapshot does not contain mastectomy/T3 content. The fact is true and is supported by the OTHER cited CancerNetwork article ('omitting-alnd-non-inferiority-fewer-arm-related-complications-breast-cancer', source-2 — 'excluded important subgroups, most notably patients undergoing mastectomy or those with larger primary tumors exceeding 5 cm', with a de Boniface quote to the same effect), as well as by The ASCO Post and OncoDaily. Recoverable misattribution to the wrong outlet/URL for a verifiable fact; filed as a correction.

content_preview.title: SENOMAC's Mature Survival Data at ASCO 2026 Confirms Many Breast Cancer Patients Can Safely Skip Axillary Dissection
content_preview.summary: Five-year overall survival was non-inferior when surgeons omitted completion axillary dissection, while severe arm problems fell from 12.6% to 3.6%.
content_preview.body_excerpt:
## Overview

Mature survival results from the international SENOMAC trial, presented at the 2026 American Society of Clinical Oncology (ASCO) Annual Meeting as abstract LBA503, found that omitting completion axillary lymph node dissection did not compromise survival in breast cancer patients with limited spread to the sentinel nodes, while sharply reducing long-term arm problems. After a median follow-up of 60.1 months, five-year overall survival was 93.4% in the group that underwent completion ...

editor_notes.content_quality: Clear, well-structured clinical report using the house News template (Overview / What We Know / Why It Matters / What We Don't Know). 633 words, within the News 400-1200 range. Appropriate technical depth for a phase III oncology trial readout; the limitations section is honest about the radiotherapy-confounded population and pending longer follow-up.
editor_notes.source_verification: All 4 source snapshots read from disk and verified (Rule 9 clinical scrutiny applied). source-0 (The ASCO Post, HTTP 200): confirms 5-yr OS 93.4% vs 94.4%, median follow-up 60.1 months, BCSS 97.2% vs 97.9%, Lymph-ICF mean difference 10.75 points, abstract LBA503, Jana de Boniface (lead investigator, Capio St. Goran's Hospital / Karolinska Institutet), and the direct quote verbatim ('...axillary surgery should be seen as a diagnostic instrument, not a therapeutic tool'). source-1 (CancerNetwork, HTTP 200): confirms HR 0.89 (95% CI 0.67-1.17), non-inferiority P<.001, OS 93.4%/94.4%, severe arm dysfunction 12.6% vs 3.6% (statistically significant), regional radiotherapy 87.9% vs 89.7%. source-2 (CancerNetwork, HTTP 200): confirms median age 61, eligibility (1-2 sentinel macrometastases >2 mm), Jane Lowe Meisel (Winship Cancer Institute, Emory) and lymphedema framing, and the mastectomy/larger-tumor de-escalation context. source-3 (OncoDaily, HTTP 200): confirms 2,766 randomized across five countries (Jan 2015-Dec 2021), per-protocol 2,540 (1,205 ALND / 1,335 omission), 196 deaths incl. 75 breast-cancer deaths, non-inferiority margin (HR <1.44, upper one-sided 90% CI).
editor_notes.factual_accuracy: Every statistic in the headline, summary, and Overview lead traces verbatim to a fetchable allowlisted snapshot. CRITICAL Rule 9/5i checks: NEJM (NEJMoa2313487) and the ASCO/JCO abstract (ascopubs.org LBA503) both 403'd and are NOT cited inline or in article.sources — confirmed zero occurrences. Data-conflict resolution verified: OncoDaily's snapshot reports HR 0.84/0.86, but the article correctly uses HR 0.89 cited to CancerNetwork, matching The ASCO Post and both CancerNetwork pieces; the rejected 0.84 does not appear anywhere in the article. Sound editorial judgment by the contributor. One body-level misattribution (see findings): the mastectomy/T3 broadened-populations claim is cited to the wrong CancerNetwork article; the fact itself is accurate and present in the other cited sources.
editor_notes.tone_assessment: Neutral and clinical throughout. No sensationalism, no AI self-reference. Attribution phrases ('according to', 'reported') used consistently.
editor_notes.originality: Original. The only adjacent prior coverage is the June 4 OPTIMA article (gene-expression test sparing chemotherapy) — a different breast-cancer de-escalation story, not SENOMAC or axillary surgery. No duplicate. Added as a curated related article on the de-escalation theme.
concerns: One body-level cite (mastectomy/T3 broadened populations) points to a CancerNetwork URL whose snapshot does not carry that content; the fact is true and supported by the article's other cited sources. Recoverable via corrections record.
recommendations: For future submissions, when two URLs from the same outlet are cited, ensure each specific claim links to the exact article that carries it.
editor_notes.overall_assessment: High-quality, source-faithful clinical report that survives rigorous Rule 9 verification: no reliance on the 403'd NEJM/ascopubs primaries, every statistic carried verbatim by a fetchable allowlisted outlet, the HR data conflict correctly resolved to 0.89, and the de Boniface quote verbatim with the correct speaker. The sole issue is a single wrong-outlet cite for a verifiable body claim — honestly summarizable in one correction. Verdict: APPROVE_WITH_CORRECTIONS.
